source_name: The Markandeya Purana, translated by F. Eden Pargiter (1904)
revision_note: Newly added with 8 verified chunks under public domain (2026-07-23).

content.text: Harishchandra ruled Ayodhya with justice and piety, having vowed that he would never speak a lie under any circumstances, gaining reknown for his truthfulness.
content.source_name: The Markandeya Purana, translated by F. Eden Pargiter (1904)

content.text: To fulfill a promise made in a dream and tested by Sage Vishvamitra, Harishchandra gladly gave away his kingdom, wealth, and empire, walking out as a pauper to preserve his integrity.
content.source_name: The Markandeya Purana, translated by F. Eden Pargiter (1904)

content.text: To pay the ritual fee (dakshina) demanded by the sage, Harishchandra sold his wife Shaivya and young son Rohitashva into slavery to a brahmin, bearing the pain of separation.
content.source_name: The Markandeya Purana, translated by F. Eden Pargiter (1904)

content.text: To pay the remaining fee, the King sold himself to an outcaste who managed the cremation grounds, performing the menial duty of collecting shroud-fees from the dead.
content.source_name: The Markandeya Purana, translated by F. Eden Pargiter (1904)

content.text: When his son died of a snakebite and Shaivya brought the body for cremation, Harishchandra refused to burn the body without the standard fee, prioritizing his duty and truth over personal grief.
content.source_name: The Markandeya Purana, translated by F. Eden Pargiter (1904)

content.text: Harishchandra taught that Dharma is rooted in Truth. A kingdom may fall and life may perish, but Truth must never be abandoned under any pressure or trial.
content.source_name: The Markandeya Purana, translated by F. Eden Pargiter (1904)

content.text: Impressed by his absolute integrity, the gods appeared, restored his son to life, and invited Harishchandra and his loyal subjects to ascend to the heavens in glory.
content.source_name: The Markandeya Purana, translated by F. Eden Pargiter (1904)

content.text: This entry compiles the translation from F. Eden Pargiter's "The Markandeya Purana" (1904), published by the Asiatic Society of Bengal, which is in the public domain.
content.source_name: The Markandeya Purana, translated by F. Eden Pargiter (1904)
